title: Effects of Disease on Drug Concentration and Response

items.title: Why renally excreted drugs need dose reduction in kidney disease
items.content:
When a drug or its metabolites are primarily excreted by the kidney and higher levels cause toxicity (high-risk pharmacokinetics), doses must be reduced in renal dysfunction.
Digoxin is one example; the antiarrhythmics dofetilide and sotalol undergo predominant renal excretion and risk QT prolongation and arrhythmias if not reduced.
Most dose adjustments use published recommendations based on creatinine clearance.
Any such modification is a first approximation and should be refined by plasma concentration data (if available) and clinical observation.
keyPoints: Reduce renally excreted drugs in renal failure (digoxin; dofetilide/sotalol → QT risk)
Adjustments are based on creatinine clearance
Treat published adjustments as a first approximation; refine with levels and clinical response
items.reference: RENAL DISEASE: "If a drug or its metabolites are primarily excreted through the kidneys and increased drug levels are associated with ADRs...drug dosages must be reduced in patients with renal dysfunction to avoid toxicity."

items.title: Why meperidine is hazardous in renal failure despite hepatic metabolism
items.content:
A parent drug can be safe in renal disease while its metabolite is not.
Meperidine undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism, so renal failure has little effect on its plasma concentration.
However, its metabolite normeperidine does undergo renal excretion, accumulates in renal failure, and probably accounts for the CNS excitation — irritability, twitching, and seizures — seen when multiple doses are given to patients with renal disease.
Protein binding of some drugs (e.g., phenytoin) may be altered in uremia, so measuring free drug concentration may be desirable.
keyPoints: Meperidine: hepatic metabolism, but normeperidine is renally cleared and accumulates
Normeperidine causes CNS excitation (irritability, twitching, seizures) in renal failure
Uremia alters protein binding (e.g., phenytoin) — consider free drug levels
items.reference: RENAL DISEASE: "its metabolite, normeperidine, does undergo renal excretion, accumulates in renal failure, and probably accounts for the signs of CNS excitation, such as irritability, twitching, and seizures..."

items.title: Why high-first-pass drugs need lower oral doses in cirrhosis
items.content:
Liver disease changes drug handling in ways standard tests cannot predict.
Standard tests of liver function are not useful in adjusting doses in diseases like hepatitis or cirrhosis.
First-pass metabolism may decrease — from disrupted hepatocyte function, altered architecture, and portacaval shunts — leading to increased oral bioavailability; the oral bioavailability of high first-pass drugs such as morphine, meperidine, midazolam, and nifedipine is almost doubled in cirrhosis.
Therefore, the size of the oral dose of such drugs should be reduced in this setting.
keyPoints: Standard LFTs do not guide dosing in liver disease
Reduced first-pass metabolism (shunts, hepatocyte dysfunction) ≈ doubles oral bioavailability
Reduce oral doses of high first-pass drugs (morphine, meperidine, midazolam, nifedipine) in cirrhosis
items.reference: LIVER DISEASE: "The oral bioavailability for high first-pass drugs such as morphine, meperidine, midazolam, and nifedipine is almost doubled in patients with cirrhosis, compared to those with normal liver function."

items.title: How decreased perfusion in heart failure and shock alters drug handling
items.content:
Hemodynamic compromise affects distribution, clearance, and absorption simultaneously.
Under decreased tissue perfusion, cardiac output is redistributed to preserve flow to heart and brain at the expense of other tissues, so drugs distribute into a smaller volume of distribution, plasma concentrations rise, and the well-perfused brain and heart are exposed to higher concentrations, increasing CNS or cardiac effects.
Decreased perfusion of the kidney and liver may impair drug clearance.
Decreased gut perfusion may reduce drug absorption, leading to reduced or absent effects of oral therapies.
keyPoints: Redistribution → smaller volume of distribution → higher plasma levels reaching brain/heart
Reduced renal/hepatic perfusion impairs clearance
Reduced gut perfusion impairs absorption of oral drugs
items.reference: HEART FAILURE AND SHOCK: "drugs may be distributed into a smaller volume of distribution, higher drug concentrations will be present in the plasma, and the tissues that are best perfused (the brain and heart) will be exposed to these higher concentrations..."

items.title: Why drugs should be started low and slow in the elderly
items.content:
Aging compounds several sources of drug variability at once.
Multiple pathologies and medications result in more drug interactions and ADRs, and aging changes the function of organs involved in drug disposition.
Initial doses should be less than the usual adult dosage and increased slowly, and the number of medications and doses per day should be kept as low as possible.
ADRs are especially common in the elderly — for example, long half-life benzodiazepines are linked to hip fractures (reflecting both fall risk from sedation and the increased incidence of osteoporosis).
keyPoints: Start below the usual adult dose and titrate slowly; minimize number of drugs/doses
Altered disposition + polypharmacy + comorbidity → frequent ADRs
Long half-life benzodiazepines → hip fractures (sedation/falls + osteoporosis)
items.reference: DRUG USE IN THE ELDERLY: "Initial doses should be less than the usual adult dosage and should be increased slowly. The number of medications, and doses per day, should be kept as low as possible."

items.title: How pediatric dosing is approximated when age-specific data are lacking
items.content:
Children are not simply small adults, but evidence to individualize dosing is sparse.
Drug metabolism and response pathways mature at different rates after birth, and the relative size of body compartments and organ function change during development.
However, there are few studies providing solid evidence to guide pediatric dosing.
In practice, doses are adjusted for size (weight or body surface area) as a first approximation unless age-specific data are available.
keyPoints: Metabolic/response pathways and body compartments mature at different rates
Pediatric dosing evidence is limited
Default to size-based (weight or BSA) dosing unless age-specific data exist
items.reference: DRUG USE IN CHILDREN: "in practice, doses are adjusted for size (weight or body surface area) as a first approximation unless age-specific data are available."

items.title: How a meticulous drug history prevents missed interactions
items.content:
Interactions belong in the differential diagnosis of any unusual response during therapy.
Patients often arrive with a legacy of drugs acquired from multiple physicians who may not know all their medications, so a meticulous drug history should list all medications — including agents not often volunteered, such as OTC drugs, health-food supplements, and topical agents like eye drops.
Certain drugs consistently risk interactions by inhibiting or inducing specific elimination pathways: CYP2D6, CYP3A, and P-glycoprotein inhibitors, and CYP3A/P-glycoprotein inducers.
Prescribers must be especially alert when these drugs are started or stopped.
keyPoints: Consider drug interaction for any unusual response on therapy
Take a full history including OTC, supplements, and topical agents (e.g., eye drops)
Be alert when starting/stopping CYP2D6/CYP3A/P-gp inhibitors or CYP3A/P-gp inducers
items.reference: INTERACTIONS BETWEEN DRUGS: "A meticulous drug history should list all medications, including agents not often volunteered during questioning, such as OTC drugs, health food supplements, and topical agents such as eye drops."

items.title: Why failure of efficacy may be the most common 'adverse' drug effect
items.content:
ADRs can mimic disease and span every organ, complicating diagnosis.
The morbidity and mortality from ADRs may be mistaken for signs of underlying disease because they can involve every organ and system.
Moreover, drug therapy for chronic conditions such as psychiatric disease or hypertension fails to achieve the desired goal in up to half of treated patients.
Thus, the most common 'adverse' drug effect may be failure of efficacy.
keyPoints: ADRs affect every organ and can be mistaken for underlying disease
Chronic therapy fails the goal in up to half of patients (e.g., psychiatric disease, hypertension)
Failure of efficacy may be the most common 'adverse' effect
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "drug therapy for chronic conditions such as psychiatric disease or hypertension does not achieve the desired goal in up to half of treated patients; thus, the most common \"adverse\" drug effect may be failure of efficacy."

items.title: How Type A and Type B adverse drug reactions differ
items.content:
Classifying an ADR guides prediction and management.
Type A reactions result from exaggeration of an intended pharmacologic action — such as increased bleeding with anticoagulants or marrow suppression with antineoplastics — and tend to be dose-dependent.
Type B reactions result from toxic effects unrelated to the intended pharmacologic action; they are often unanticipated (especially with new drugs), frequently severe, often immunologic, may occur at low dosages, and are termed dose-independent.
Because trials enroll relatively few, less sick patients, rare serious ADRs are generally not detected before approval.
keyPoints: Type A: exaggerated intended pharmacology, dose-dependent (e.g., anticoagulant bleeding)
Type B: toxicity unrelated to intended action, often immunologic, dose-independent
Rare serious ADRs usually escape detection in pre-approval trials
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Type A reactions result from exaggeration of an intended pharmacologic action of the drug...Type B reactions result from toxic effects unrelated to the intended pharmacologic actions."

items.title: How adverse effects have been repurposed into new indications
items.content:
An unwanted effect can occasionally become a new therapy.
Unwanted hair growth during minoxidil treatment of severely hypertensive patients led to development of the drug for hair growth.
Sildenafil was initially developed as an antianginal, but its effect to alleviate erectile dysfunction led to a new drug indication and to increased understanding of the role of type 5 phosphodiesterase in erectile tissue.
Elucidating ADR mechanisms can also yield safer compounds or identify high-risk patient subsets to exclude from exposure.
keyPoints: Minoxidil (antihypertensive) → repurposed for hair growth
Sildenafil (antianginal) → erectile dysfunction (PDE5 biology)
Understanding ADR mechanisms aids safer drugs and risk-subset exclusion
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Unwanted hair growth during minoxidil treatment of severely hypertensive patients led to development of the drug for hair growth. Sildenafil was initially developed as an antianginal..."

items.title: How large the adverse-drug-reaction burden is
items.content:
ADRs are a major, partly preventable cause of hospital harm.
One UK estimate found that 6.5% of all hospital admissions are due to ADRs and that 0.15% of these patients died as a result.
The most common culprit drugs were aspirin, NSAIDs, diuretics, warfarin, ACE inhibitors, antidepressants, opiates, digoxin, steroids, and clopidogrel.
A U.S. study suggested ADRs were responsible for >100,000 in-hospital deaths, making them among the fourth-to-sixth most common causes of in-hospital death, with no improvement a decade later.
keyPoints: UK: ~6.5% of admissions due to ADRs; ~0.15% of those died
Top culprits: aspirin, NSAIDs, diuretics, warfarin, ACE inhibitors, antidepressants, opiates, digoxin, steroids, clopidogrel
US: ADRs implicated in >100,000 in-hospital deaths
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "One estimate in the United Kingdom was that 6.5% of all hospital admissions are due to ADRs and that 2.3% of these patients (0.15%) died as a result."

items.title: Why acetaminophen hepatotoxicity depends on glutathione
items.content:
Some toxicity arises from reactive metabolites rather than the parent drug's primary action.
Reactive metabolites generated by CYPs can covalently bind tissue macromolecules close to their site of production, typically the liver.
In acetaminophen overdose, reactive metabolites are normally detoxified by combining with hepatic glutathione; when glutathione is depleted, the metabolites bind hepatic protein, causing hepatocyte damage — preventable or attenuated by N-acetylcysteine.
The risk is increased by drugs that induce metabolism (phenobarbital, phenytoin) or by ethanol, which exhausts glutathione stores.
keyPoints: CYP-generated reactive metabolites bind macromolecules near their site (usually liver)
Acetaminophen: glutathione detoxifies the metabolite; depletion → hepatocyte damage; treat with N-acetylcysteine
Risk increased by enzyme inducers (phenobarbital, phenytoin) and by ethanol (depletes glutathione)
items.reference: Acetaminophen: "When glutathione becomes depleted, the metabolites bind instead to hepatic protein, with resultant hepatocyte damage."

items.title: How drugs trigger antibody-mediated cytopenias
items.content:
Drug-induced immune reactions destroy blood cells by several distinct mechanisms.
An antibody may attack a drug covalently attached to a cell and destroy it, as in penicillin-induced hemolytic anemia.
Antibody-drug-antigen complexes may be passively adsorbed onto a bystander cell that is then destroyed by complement, as in quinine- and quinidine-induced thrombocytopenia.
Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia arises when antibodies against platelet factor 4-heparin complexes form immune complexes that activate platelets — so the thrombocytopenia is accompanied by 'paradoxical' thrombosis and is treated with thrombin inhibitors.
keyPoints: Penicillin: antibody attacks drug bound to red cells → hemolytic anemia
Quinine/quinidine: bystander cell destroyed by complement → thrombocytopenia
HIT: anti-PF4/heparin complexes activate platelets → thrombocytopenia WITH thrombosis (treat with thrombin inhibitors)
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia arises when antibodies against complexes of platelet factor 4 peptide and heparin generate immune complexes that activate platelets; thus, the thrombocytopenia is accompanied by \"paradoxical\" thrombosis and is treated with thrombin inhibitors."

items.title: How drug-induced lupus, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions arise
items.content:
Drugs can provoke autoimmunity, IgE-mediated anaphylaxis, and T-cell-mediated skin loss.
Drugs or reactive metabolites may render a host tissue antigenic and elicit autoantibodies, as in hydralazine- or procainamide-induced lupus erythematosus.
Many drugs — particularly antimicrobials, ACE inhibitors, and aspirin — can elicit anaphylaxis with IgE that binds mast cells, releasing mediators that cause urticaria, wheezing, flushing, rhinorrhea, and occasionally hypotension.
Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis is a T-cell-mediated reaction that can cause death from massive skin sloughing, and DRESS is a rare relapsing reaction often triggered by antiseizure medications.
keyPoints: Hydralazine/procainamide → drug-induced lupus (autoantibodies)
Antimicrobials/ACE inhibitors/aspirin → IgE-mediated anaphylaxis
SJS/TEN = T-cell-mediated skin sloughing (can be fatal); DRESS often triggered by antiseizure drugs
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "One serious reaction is Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN), which can result in death due to T-cell-mediated massive skin sloughing."

items.title: Why immune checkpoint inhibitors cause immune-related adverse events
items.content:
Releasing the immune system's brakes treats cancer but creates a new class of toxicity.
While antibodies targeting immune checkpoints are dramatically improving prognosis in many cancers, they have also been associated with the unpredictable development of many apparently immune-related ADRs.
Some, like colitis or thyroiditis, may be self-limited or medically manageable.
Others, notably myocarditis, are rarer but can be rapidly fatal.
keyPoints: Checkpoint inhibitors cause unpredictable immune-related ADRs
Colitis and thyroiditis are often self-limited or manageable
Myocarditis is rare but can be rapidly fatal
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "Some, like colitis or thyroiditis, may be self-limited or medically manageable, while others, notably myocarditis, are rarer but can be rapidly fatal."

items.title: Why a drug interaction can masquerade as a reaction to a newly added drug
items.content:
A new symptom after starting a drug does not always reflect direct toxicity of that drug.
It is important to remember that a drug interaction may be responsible: a patient on a chronic stable warfarin dose may bleed after amiodarone is introduced, not as a direct reaction to amiodarone but because amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism.
Specific effects such as hypoglycemia with insulin or bleeding with anticoagulants are more readily linked to a drug than nonspecific symptoms such as rash; drug fever often escapes diagnosis because fever is a common manifestation of disease.
Thus the newly added drug may be the trigger of an interaction rather than the culprit itself.
keyPoints: Consider interaction, not just direct toxicity, when a new drug is added (e.g., amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism → bleeding)
Specific effects (insulin hypoglycemia, anticoagulant bleeding) are easier to attribute than rash
Drug fever is easily missed because fever is common in disease
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "a patient on a chronic stable warfarin dose may develop a bleeding complication after introduction of amiodarone; this does not reflect a direct reaction to amiodarone but rather its effect to inhibit warfarin metabolism."

items.title: How dechallenge and rechallenge are used (and when rechallenge is avoided)
items.content:
Stopping and, rarely, restarting a drug helps establish causality.
Once an ADR is suspected, discontinuation of the suspected drug followed by disappearance of the reaction is presumptive evidence of a drug-induced illness.
Confirming evidence may be sought by cautiously reintroducing the drug to see if the reaction reappears — but rechallenge should be done only if the suspected culprit is critical to the patient's care, and when the reaction is thought to be immunologic, challenge is generally avoided.
The time for a concentration-dependent ADR to disappear depends on the time for the concentration to fall below the offending range, so effects of long half-life drugs may take considerable time to resolve.
keyPoints: Dechallenge (stop → resolves) is presumptive evidence of a drug cause
Rechallenge only if the drug is critical; avoid it for immunologic reactions
Resolution time depends on half-life — long half-life ADRs resolve slowly
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "However, rechallenge should be done only if the suspected culprit drug is critical to the patient's care. When the reaction is thought to be immunologic, challenge is generally avoided."

items.title: Why single-target 'magic bullets' often give way to combination therapy
items.content:
Targeting one molecule can produce spectacular but short-lived responses.
Many modern therapies begin from basic discovery of target molecules (e.g., HMG-CoA reductase, the BRAF V600E mutation driving some melanomas).
Initial spectacular successes with vemurafenib (targeting BRAF V600E) were followed by near-universal tumor relapse, strongly suggesting that inhibiting this pathway alone is insufficient for tumor control.
This supports the view that many complex diseases require single drugs or combinations attacking multiple pathways — as seen with combination therapy in hypertension, tuberculosis, HIV infection, and many cancers.
keyPoints: Drug discovery often starts from a validated target molecule
Vemurafenib (BRAF V600E): dramatic response but near-universal relapse with monotherapy
Complex diseases often need multi-pathway/combination therapy (HTN, TB, HIV, cancer)
items.reference: THE DRUG DEVELOPMENT PROCESS: "initial spectacular successes with vemurafenib (which targets BRAF V600E) were followed by near-universal tumor relapse, strongly suggesting that inhibition of this pathway alone would be insufficient for tumor control."

items.title: How a lead compound progresses through phases 1 to 3
items.content:
Modern development moves from screening to staged human testing.
A common approach starts with high-throughput screening to identify 'lead' chemicals modulating a target, followed by medicinal-chemistry refinement for target specificity, lack of off-target effects, and suitable pharmacokinetics (consistent bioavailability, long half-life, no high-risk pharmacokinetic features).
Human evaluation then proceeds from initial safety and tolerance (phase 1) to dose finding (phase 2) and large efficacy trials (phase 3).
The process is very expensive and most lead compounds fail; drugs whose development includes supporting human genetic data have a decreased chance of failing.
keyPoints: Pipeline: high-throughput screen → lead → medicinal chemistry → phase 1 (safety) → 2 (dose) → 3 (efficacy)
Aim for specificity, no off-target effects, and good PK (no high-risk features)
Human genetic support for the target lowers the chance of development failure
items.reference: THE DRUG DEVELOPMENT PROCESS: "Drug evaluation in human subjects then proceeds from initial safety and tolerance (phase 1) to dose finding (phase 2) and then to large efficacy trials (phase 3)."

items.title: Why no drug response should ever be called idiosyncratic
items.content:
Modern clinical pharmacology replaces empiricism with mechanism.
No drug response should ever be termed idiosyncratic; all responses have a mechanism whose understanding will help guide further therapy with that drug.
Fundamental prescribing principles follow: the benefits of drug therapy should outweigh the risk; the smallest dosage necessary to produce the desired effect should be used; and the number of medications and doses per day should be minimized.
Prescribers should be especially wary when adding or stopping drugs liable to provoke interactions and ADRs, and should use only a limited number of drugs with which they are thoroughly familiar.
keyPoints: Every drug response has a mechanism — 'idiosyncratic' is never an adequate explanation
Benefit should outweigh risk; use the smallest effective dose; minimize drugs/doses
Be cautious adding/stopping interaction-prone drugs; master a limited formulary
items.reference: SUMMARY: "No drug response should ever be termed idiosyncratic; all responses have a mechanism whose understanding will help guide further therapy with that drug."

items.question: A patient with chronic kidney disease is to start an antiarrhythmic. Which drug most requires dose reduction because predominant renal excretion risks QT prolongation and arrhythmia?
options: Amiodarone
Lidocaine
Dofetilide
Verapamil
items.explanation: Dofetilide (and sotalol) undergo predominant renal excretion and risk QT prolongation/arrhythmia if doses are not reduced in renal disease; digoxin is another renally cleared example needing adjustment.
items.reference: RENAL DISEASE: "the antiarrhythmics dofetilide and sotalol, which undergo predominant renal excretion and carry a risk of QT prolongation and arrhythmias if doses are not reduced in renal disease."

items.question: A patient with renal failure receives repeated doses of meperidine and develops irritability, twitching, and a seizure. The best explanation is accumulation of:
options: Meperidine itself, because renal failure raises its plasma level
Normeperidine, a renally excreted metabolite that accumulates in renal failure
Glucuronide conjugates that are hepatotoxic
Free phenytoin displaced by meperidine
items.explanation: Meperidine is extensively hepatically metabolized, but its metabolite normeperidine is renally excreted, accumulates in renal failure, and causes CNS excitation (irritability, twitching, seizures).
items.reference: RENAL DISEASE: "its metabolite, normeperidine, does undergo renal excretion, accumulates in renal failure, and probably accounts for the signs of CNS excitation, such as irritability, twitching, and seizures..."

items.question: Why should the oral dose of a high-first-pass drug such as midazolam or nifedipine be reduced in a patient with cirrhosis?
options: Cirrhosis increases renal excretion of these drugs
Reduced first-pass metabolism (from shunts and hepatocyte dysfunction) almost doubles oral bioavailability
Cirrhosis increases protein binding, raising total drug levels
These drugs are not metabolized by the liver
items.explanation: Decreased first-pass metabolism in cirrhosis — from disrupted hepatocyte function, altered architecture, and portacaval shunts — almost doubles the oral bioavailability of high first-pass drugs (morphine, meperidine, midazolam, nifedipine), so the oral dose should be reduced.
items.reference: LIVER DISEASE: "The oral bioavailability for high first-pass drugs such as morphine, meperidine, midazolam, and nifedipine is almost doubled in patients with cirrhosis..."

items.question: Which statement about adjusting drug doses in liver disease is correct?
options: Standard liver function tests reliably predict the needed dose adjustment
Standard liver function tests are not useful for adjusting doses in hepatitis or cirrhosis
Liver disease never affects oral bioavailability
Doses should always be increased in cirrhosis
items.explanation: Standard tests of liver function are not useful in adjusting doses in diseases like hepatitis or cirrhosis; instead, knowledge of first-pass kinetics guides dose reduction for high-first-pass drugs.
items.reference: LIVER DISEASE: "Standard tests of liver function are not useful in adjusting doses in diseases like hepatitis or cirrhosis."

items.question: In a patient in shock, why might a standard intravenous loading dose of a drug produce exaggerated CNS or cardiac effects?
options: Increased volume of distribution dilutes the drug
Redistribution of cardiac output gives a smaller volume of distribution and higher plasma levels reaching the well-perfused brain and heart
Enhanced renal clearance lowers plasma levels
Gut perfusion is increased, accelerating absorption
items.explanation: Decreased perfusion redistributes cardiac output to heart and brain, so the drug distributes into a smaller volume of distribution, plasma concentrations rise, and the well-perfused brain and heart are exposed to higher concentrations.
items.reference: HEART FAILURE AND SHOCK: "drugs may be distributed into a smaller volume of distribution, higher drug concentrations will be present in the plasma, and the tissues that are best perfused (the brain and heart) will be exposed to these higher concentrations..."

items.question: Which prescribing principle is most appropriate when initiating drug therapy in an elderly patient?
options: Begin at the usual adult dose and escalate rapidly
Start below the usual adult dose and increase slowly, minimizing the number of medications
Avoid all dose adjustment because aging does not affect disposition
Use long half-life benzodiazepines preferentially for safety
items.explanation: In the elderly, initial doses should be less than the usual adult dosage and increased slowly, with the number of medications and doses per day kept as low as possible. Long half-life benzodiazepines are linked to hip fractures.
items.reference: DRUG USE IN THE ELDERLY: "Initial doses should be less than the usual adult dosage and should be increased slowly."

items.question: Long half-life benzodiazepines in elderly patients have been specifically linked to which adverse outcome?
options: Hepatic failure
Hip fractures
QT prolongation
Hemolytic anemia
items.explanation: Use of long half-life benzodiazepines is linked to hip fractures in the elderly, reflecting both fall risk from increased sedation and the increased incidence of osteoporosis in this group.
items.reference: DRUG USE IN THE ELDERLY: "use of long half-life benzodiazepines is linked to the occurrence of hip fractures in elderly patients..."

items.question: In the absence of age-specific pediatric data, drug doses for children are generally approximated by:
options: Using the full adult dose
Adjusting for size (weight or body surface area)
Halving the adult dose regardless of age
Using plasma genotyping to set the dose
items.explanation: Because pediatric dosing evidence is limited, doses are adjusted for size (weight or body surface area) as a first approximation unless age-specific data are available.
items.reference: DRUG USE IN CHILDREN: "doses are adjusted for size (weight or body surface area) as a first approximation unless age-specific data are available."

items.question: Which classes of drugs most consistently generate interactions and warrant special vigilance when started or stopped?
options: Topical emollients and antacids
CYP2D6, CYP3A, and P-glycoprotein inhibitors, and CYP3A/P-glycoprotein inducers
Intravenous fluids and electrolytes
Inhaled bronchodilators only
items.explanation: Certain drugs consistently risk interactions by inhibiting or inducing specific elimination pathways: CYP2D6, CYP3A, and P-glycoprotein inhibitors, and CYP3A/P-glycoprotein inducers. Prescribers must be especially alert when these are started or stopped.
items.reference: INTERACTIONS BETWEEN DRUGS: "these include CYP2D6, CYP3A, and P-glycoprotein inhibitors...and CYP3A/P-glycoprotein inducers...when these drugs are started or stopped, prescribers must be especially alert to the possibility of interactions."

items.question: Increased bleeding from an anticoagulant is best classified as which type of adverse drug reaction?
options: Type A — exaggeration of the intended pharmacologic action, dose-dependent
Type B — toxicity unrelated to the intended action, dose-independent
An immunologic (allergic) reaction
An idiosyncratic reaction with no mechanism
items.explanation: Increased bleeding with anticoagulants is a Type A reaction — an exaggeration of the intended pharmacologic effect — and tends to be dose-dependent. Type B reactions are unrelated to the intended action and often immunologic.
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Type A reactions result from exaggeration of an intended pharmacologic action of the drug, such as increased bleeding with anticoagulants...and these tend to be dose-dependent."

items.question: Why are rare serious adverse drug reactions generally not detected before a drug is approved?
options: Regulators ignore rare events
Pre-approval trials study relatively few, less sick, selected patients, so rare ADRs go undetected
Rare ADRs do not occur in humans
All ADRs are dose-dependent and predictable
items.explanation: New drugs are tested in relatively few patients who are less sick with fewer comorbidities than those who later receive the drug; given the small, selected samples, rare serious ADRs are generally not detected before approval — hence caution with new drugs.
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Because of the relatively small number of patients studied in clinical trials and the selected nature of these patients, rare serious ADRs are generally not detected prior to a drug's approval..."

items.question: Which factor increases the risk of acetaminophen-induced hepatic necrosis?
options: Concurrent N-acetylcysteine
Chronic ethanol use, which exhausts glutathione stores
A high glutathione reserve
Renal excretion of acetaminophen
items.explanation: Acetaminophen hepatotoxicity occurs when glutathione is depleted and reactive metabolites bind hepatic protein. Risk is increased by enzyme inducers (phenobarbital, phenytoin) and by ethanol, which exhausts glutathione. N-acetylcysteine is protective.
items.reference: Acetaminophen: "The risk of acetaminophen-related hepatic necrosis is increased in patients receiving drugs such as phenobarbital or phenytoin, which increase the rate of drug metabolism, or ethanol, which exhausts glutathione stores."

items.question: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is characteristically accompanied by which paradoxical feature, and how is it treated?
options: Bleeding; treated with platelet transfusion
Thrombosis; treated with thrombin inhibitors
Hemolysis; treated with corticosteroids
Neutropenia; treated with G-CSF
items.explanation: In HIT, antibodies against PF4-heparin complexes activate platelets, so thrombocytopenia is accompanied by paradoxical thrombosis; it is treated with thrombin inhibitors (not more heparin or platelet transfusion).
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "the thrombocytopenia is accompanied by \"paradoxical\" thrombosis and is treated with thrombin inhibitors."

items.question: Hydralazine and procainamide are classically associated with which immune-mediated adverse reaction?
options: Drug-induced lupus erythematosus
Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Anaphylaxis
Serum sickness
items.explanation: Drugs or reactive metabolites may render host tissue antigenic and elicit autoantibodies; hydralazine- or procainamide-induced lupus erythematosus is the classic example.
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "hydralazine- or procainamide-induced lupus erythematosus is an example."

items.question: Among immune-related adverse events from checkpoint inhibitors, which is rare but can be rapidly fatal?
options: Thyroiditis
Colitis
Myocarditis
Vitiligo
items.explanation: While colitis and thyroiditis are often self-limited or manageable, myocarditis is a rarer immune-related adverse event that can be rapidly fatal.
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "others, notably myocarditis, are rarer but can be rapidly fatal."

items.question: A patient stable on warfarin develops bleeding shortly after amiodarone is started. The most likely mechanism is:
options: A direct toxic effect of amiodarone on platelets
Amiodarone inhibiting warfarin metabolism, raising warfarin levels
An IgE-mediated allergy to amiodarone
Renal accumulation of warfarin
items.explanation: This is a drug interaction, not a direct reaction: amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism, raising warfarin concentration and causing bleeding. Interactions must be considered when a new drug is added to a stable regimen.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "this does not reflect a direct reaction to amiodarone but rather its effect to inhibit warfarin metabolism."

items.question: When is rechallenge with a suspected culprit drug appropriate after an adverse drug reaction?
options: Routinely, to confirm every ADR
Only if the suspected drug is critical to the patient's care, and generally avoided for immunologic reactions
Always for immunologic reactions, to characterize them
Never, under any circumstance
items.explanation: Dechallenge (stopping the drug with resolution) is presumptive evidence; cautious rechallenge confirms causality but should be done only if the drug is critical to care, and challenge is generally avoided when the reaction is thought to be immunologic.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "rechallenge should be done only if the suspected culprit drug is critical to the patient's care. When the reaction is thought to be immunologic, challenge is generally avoided."

items.question: The near-universal tumor relapse after initial responses to vemurafenib (a BRAF V600E inhibitor) best supports which principle of therapeutics?
options: Single-pathway targeting is usually sufficient to cure complex disease
Many complex diseases require combinations attacking multiple pathways
Targeted therapy is always inferior to chemotherapy
Genetic data are irrelevant to drug development
items.explanation: Relapse after vemurafenib monotherapy suggests inhibiting one pathway is insufficient, supporting the view that complex diseases often need single drugs or combinations attacking multiple pathways (as in HTN, TB, HIV, and many cancers).
items.reference: THE DRUG DEVELOPMENT PROCESS: "single drugs or combinations that attack multiple pathways whose perturbation results in disease."

items.question: In the modern drug-development pipeline, which phase of human testing primarily establishes initial safety and tolerance?
options: Phase 1
Phase 2
Phase 3
Postmarketing surveillance
items.explanation: Human evaluation proceeds from initial safety and tolerance (phase 1) to dose finding (phase 2) to large efficacy trials (phase 3).
items.reference: THE DRUG DEVELOPMENT PROCESS: "Drug evaluation in human subjects then proceeds from initial safety and tolerance (phase 1) to dose finding (phase 2) and then to large efficacy trials (phase 3)."

items.question: Which statement reflects a fundamental prescribing principle from modern clinical pharmacology?
options: Some drug responses are truly idiosyncratic and need no mechanistic explanation
No drug response should be termed idiosyncratic; all responses have a mechanism
The largest tolerated dose should always be used
More medications generally improve adherence and outcomes
items.explanation: A core principle is that no drug response should be termed idiosyncratic — all responses have a mechanism — alongside using the smallest effective dose and minimizing the number of medications and daily doses.
items.reference: SUMMARY: "No drug response should ever be termed idiosyncratic; all responses have a mechanism whose understanding will help guide further therapy with that drug."

items.statement: Because meperidine is extensively metabolized by the liver, renal failure has little effect on the plasma concentration of meperidine itself.
items.explanation: True — meperidine undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism, so renal failure has little effect on its own plasma level; the danger lies in accumulation of its renally cleared metabolite normeperidine.
items.reference: RENAL DISEASE: "The narcotic analgesic meperidine undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism, so that renal failure has little effect on its plasma concentration."

items.statement: Standard liver function tests reliably indicate how much to adjust drug doses in cirrhosis.
items.explanation: False — standard tests of liver function are not useful for adjusting doses in hepatitis or cirrhosis; dosing relies on knowledge of first-pass kinetics.
items.reference: LIVER DISEASE: "Standard tests of liver function are not useful in adjusting doses in diseases like hepatitis or cirrhosis."

items.statement: Severe heart failure can reduce gut perfusion and thereby reduce absorption of orally administered drugs.
items.explanation: True — decreased gut perfusion in severe heart failure may reduce drug absorption, leading to reduced or absent effects of oral therapies.
items.reference: HEART FAILURE AND SHOCK: "decreased gut perfusion, which may reduce drug absorption and thus lead to reduced or absent effects of orally administered therapies."

items.statement: Type B adverse drug reactions are typically dose-dependent exaggerations of the drug's intended pharmacologic action.
items.explanation: False — that describes Type A reactions. Type B reactions result from toxic effects unrelated to the intended action, are often immunologic, may occur at low doses, and are termed dose-independent.
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Type B reactions result from toxic effects unrelated to the intended pharmacologic actions...Type B reactions may occur at low dosages and are often termed dose-independent."

items.statement: Failure of efficacy may be the most common 'adverse' drug effect, since chronic therapy fails its goal in up to half of treated patients for some conditions.
items.explanation: True — drug therapy for chronic conditions such as psychiatric disease or hypertension does not achieve the desired goal in up to half of patients, so failure of efficacy may be the most common 'adverse' effect.
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "the most common \"adverse\" drug effect may be failure of efficacy."

items.statement: N-acetylcysteine prevents or attenuates acetaminophen hepatotoxicity by reducing binding of electrophilic metabolites to hepatic proteins.
items.explanation: True — N-acetylcysteine reduces the binding of electrophilic acetaminophen metabolites to hepatic proteins, preventing or attenuating hepatic necrosis.
items.reference: Acetaminophen: "The hepatic necrosis produced by the ingestion of acetaminophen can be prevented or attenuated by the administration of substances such as N-acetylcysteine that reduce the binding of electrophilic metabolites to hepatic proteins."

items.statement: Penicillin-induced hemolytic anemia occurs when antibody attacks the drug while it is covalently attached to the red cell, destroying the cell.
items.explanation: True — in penicillin-induced hemolytic anemia, antibody attacks the drug covalently bound to the cell, thereby destroying the cell.
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "The antibody may attack the drug when the drug is covalently attached to a cell and thereby destroy the cell. This occurs in penicillin-induced hemolytic anemia."

items.statement: Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis is mediated by T cells and can be fatal due to massive skin sloughing.
items.explanation: True — SJS/TEN results from T-cell-mediated massive skin sloughing and can result in death.
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN), which can result in death due to T-cell-mediated massive skin sloughing."

items.statement: Serious, sometimes fatal adverse reactions have been recognized with herbal and over-the-counter products, including kava-associated hepatotoxicity.
items.explanation: True — serious ADRs are well recognized with herbal and OTC compounds, including kava-associated hepatotoxicity, L-tryptophan-associated eosinophilia-myalgia, and phenylpropanolamine-associated stroke, each of which has caused fatalities.
items.reference: SCOPE OF THE ADVERSE DRUG REACTION PROBLEM: "examples include kava-associated hepatotoxicity, L-tryptophan-associated eosinophilia-myalgia, and phenylpropanol-amine-associated stroke, each of which has caused fatalities."

items.statement: Adverse effects of drugs with long half-lives may take a considerable time to disappear after the drug is stopped.
items.explanation: True — the time for a concentration-dependent ADR to disappear depends on the time for the level to fall below the offending range, so adverse effects of long half-life drugs (or those not directly related to serum concentration) may take considerable time to resolve.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Adverse effects of drugs with long half-lives or those not directly related to serum concentration may take a considerable time to disappear."

items.statement: Drugs whose development includes supporting human genetic data have a decreased chance of failing in the development process.
items.explanation: True — human genetic support for a target lowers the chance of failure, which is why genomic data are increasingly used in development.
items.reference: THE DRUG DEVELOPMENT PROCESS: "drugs whose development includes supporting human genetic data have a decreased chance of failing in the drug development process."

items.statement: A core prescribing principle is that the smallest dosage necessary to produce the desired effect should be used.
items.explanation: True — fundamental principles include that benefits should outweigh risk, the smallest effective dose should be used, and the number of medications and daily doses should be minimized.
items.reference: SUMMARY: "The smallest dosage necessary to produce the desired effect should be used."

items.assertion: Repeated doses of meperidine should be avoided in patients with renal failure.
items.reason: Its metabolite normeperidine is renally excreted and accumulates, causing CNS excitation and seizures.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: accumulation of the renally cleared metabolite normeperidine causes CNS excitation/seizures, so repeated meperidine dosing is hazardous in renal failure.
items.reference: RENAL DISEASE: "its metabolite, normeperidine, does undergo renal excretion, accumulates in renal failure, and probably accounts for the signs of CNS excitation, such as irritability, twitching, and seizures..."

items.assertion: The oral dose of midazolam should be reduced in a patient with cirrhosis.
items.reason: Reduced first-pass metabolism in cirrhosis almost doubles the oral bioavailability of high-first-pass drugs.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because cirrhosis roughly doubles oral bioavailability of high-first-pass drugs like midazolam, the oral dose should be reduced.
items.reference: LIVER DISEASE: "Therefore, the size of the oral dose of such drugs should be reduced in this setting."

items.assertion: Type B adverse drug reactions can usually be predicted from the drug's known pharmacology and avoided by lowering the dose.
items.reason: Type B reactions arise from exaggeration of the drug's intended pharmacologic action.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Type B reactions are unrelated to the intended pharmacology, are often unanticipated and immunologic, and can occur at low doses (dose-independent). The reason describes Type A, not Type B. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "Type B reactions result from toxic effects unrelated to the intended pharmacologic actions. The latter effects are often unanticipated...and Type B reactions may occur at low dosages and are often termed dose-independent."

items.assertion: Bleeding in a stable warfarin patient after starting amiodarone should be attributed to direct amiodarone toxicity.
items.reason: Amiodarone inhibits the metabolism of warfarin, raising its concentration.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — the bleeding reflects a drug interaction, not direct amiodarone toxicity. The reason is true and explains the real mechanism (inhibition of warfarin metabolism). False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "this does not reflect a direct reaction to amiodarone but rather its effect to inhibit warfarin metabolism."

items.assertion: Chronic alcohol use increases the risk of acetaminophen-induced hepatic necrosis.
items.reason: Ethanol exhausts hepatic glutathione stores needed to detoxify acetaminophen's reactive metabolite.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: by depleting glutathione, ethanol leaves more reactive metabolite to bind hepatic protein, increasing the risk of hepatic necrosis.
items.reference: Acetaminophen: "or ethanol, which exhausts glutathione stores."

items.assertion: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is treated with a thrombin inhibitor rather than with more heparin.
items.reason: HIT is accompanied by paradoxical thrombosis because the PF4-heparin antibody complexes activate platelets.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because immune complexes activate platelets and cause thrombosis, anticoagulation is switched to a thrombin inhibitor.
items.reference: Immunologic Reactions: "the thrombocytopenia is accompanied by \"paradoxical\" thrombosis and is treated with thrombin inhibitors."

items.assertion: Drugs should generally be started at lower doses in elderly patients.
items.reason: Aging changes the function of organs involved in drug disposition and elderly patients commonly take multiple drugs.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: altered disposition plus polypharmacy and comorbidity make the elderly prone to ADRs, so therapy is started low and increased slowly.
items.reference: DRUG USE IN THE ELDERLY: "Aging also results in changes in organ function, especially of the organs involved in drug disposition. Initial doses should be less than the usual adult dosage..."

items.assertion: When an immunologically mediated ADR is suspected, rechallenge with the drug is generally avoided.
items.reason: Re-exposure in an immunologic reaction can provoke a severe or dangerous response.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because immunologic re-exposure risks a severe reaction, challenge is generally avoided when the reaction is thought to be immunologic.
items.reference: DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT OF ADVERSE DRUG REACTIONS: "When the reaction is thought to be immunologic, challenge is generally avoided."

items.assertion: The drug history should include over-the-counter products, supplements, and eye drops.
items.reason: Such agents are often not volunteered by patients yet can participate in clinically important drug interactions.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because these agents are often unmentioned but can cause interactions, a meticulous history must capture them.
items.reference: INTERACTIONS BETWEEN DRUGS: "A meticulous drug history should list all medications, including agents not often volunteered during questioning, such as OTC drugs, health food supplements, and topical agents such as eye drops."

items.assertion: Targeting the BRAF V600E mutation alone reliably cures the melanomas it drives.
items.reason: Vemurafenib produces near-universal durable remissions when used as monotherapy.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. Vemurafenib (BRAF V600E inhibitor) produces spectacular but typically non-durable responses, with near-universal relapse — which is why combination therapy is needed. Both false = option 4.
items.reference: THE DRUG DEVELOPMENT PROCESS: "initial spectacular successes with vemurafenib (which targets BRAF V600E) were followed by near-universal tumor relapse."
